_meta.game: Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles IV: Turtles in Time

sources.name: Wikipedia — TMNT: Turtles in Time
sources.notes: Character differences, mechanics overview, SNES vs arcade comparison.

sources.name: TASVideos — SNES / TMNT IV Turtles in Time
sources.notes: TASVideos run documentation. Character analysis: Raphael walks faster up/down, all turtles same horizontal run speed.

notes: TMNT IV is a beat-em-up (not a platformer), but included here for its jump + combat physics which overlap with platformer paradigms.
DEFINING MECHANIC: Throw enemies INTO THE SCREEN — enemies fly at the player/camera.
4 playable characters: Leonardo, Donatello, Raphael, Michelangelo. All have same horizontal run speed but different stats.
Raphael is slightly faster at up/down maneuvering. Donatello has the longest weapon reach.
Time travel theme: stages set in ancient Rome, pirate era, prehistoric era, and futuristic Technodrome.
Based on arcade version but SNES adds additional content not in the arcade.

leonardo.notes: Balanced character. Dual swords allow slightly wider hit arc.

donatello.notes: Best range in the game. Staff hits enemies slightly farther away than other weapons. Recommended for beginners (confirmed TASVideos).

notes: Fastest character — particularly faster at up/down maneuvering (confirmed TASVideos).
Short range compensated by attack speed.
Best for aggressive players comfortable with close-range combat.

michelangelo.notes: Attack animation whips in an arc — slightly different hitbox profile than staff or katana.

movement._note: Turtles in Time is a belt-scroller — movement is in 2D scrolling space with up/down depth axis.

horizontal_run_speed.notes: All four turtles have the same horizontal run speed. Raphael's advantage is specifically up/down (depth) movement.

vertical_depth_speed.raphael_advantage: Raphael moves faster up/down in the belt-scroller depth axis.
vertical_depth_speed.notes: Useful for repositioning to target enemies at different depths or avoiding attacks.

dash.description: Double-tap direction OR set to 'Manual' mode in options.
notes: Manual dash mode is faster for speedruns — allows sprinting from any position in only a few frames.
Running attacks are significantly more powerful than standing attacks.
Setting to Manual in options is the standard speedrun setup.

jump.notes: Jumping into enemies deals contact damage. Jump attacks are different from ground attacks.

basic_attack.description: Press Y for basic attack combo. Each turtle has a 3-4 hit combo string.
basic_attack.notes: Attack animations are weapon-specific — staff has different arc than sai.

jump_attack.description: Attack while airborne. Typically a downward or horizontal swing.
jump_attack.notes: Jump attacks hit airborne enemies more reliably.

super_attack.description: Full-screen special attack. Costs health to execute.
super_attack.notes: Emergency crowd-clear. Expensive — trades HP for damage output.

throw_into_screen._note: THE DEFINING MECHANIC of Turtles in Time.
throw_into_screen.description: Grab an enemy and throw them toward the screen (at the player/camera). The enemy flies toward the viewer and hits the edge of the screen.
throw_into_screen.effect: Instant defeat of the thrown enemy. Can hit other enemies in its path.
throw_into_screen.activation: Grab enemy, then press toward screen (down+Y or specific input — VERIFY exact input)
notes: The screen-throw is visually striking — enemies fly toward the camera and hit the 'glass.'
This technique was unique to Turtles in Time and became one of the game's signature mechanics.
Different from a standard throw which tosses enemies in-plane.
Some boss enemies cannot be screen-thrown.

level_design._note: Time travel theme creates diverse historical/futuristic environments within one game.
stages: 1990 New York — Present day, Foot Clan assault
1530 AD Pirate period — Slash as boss
Future City / Technodrome — Future TMNT setting
level_design.notes: Each stage uses the same physics engine with different visual themes. The variety is aesthetic, not mechanical — unlike Battletoads which changed gameplay modes.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: The screen-throw mechanic breaks the fourth wall in a physical sense — the enemy trajectory implies the camera is a real surface. It makes the player feel like they're in the world rather than watching through a window. This is a rare example of physics-based fourth-wall interaction.

Character differentiation in Turtles in Time is minimal by design — all turtles play similarly with minor stat differences. This is appropriate for a co-op game: meaningful choices without forcing one player to pick a 'bad' character. The differences are style preferences, not power rankings.

Donatello's reach advantage is exactly the kind of single-stat difference that creates meaningful diversity without unbalancing: range matters most at the edge of engagement. In a crowd, it's irrelevant. Against a single enemy, it's decisive. Context-dependent advantage.

Turtles in Time demonstrates that the same physics engine can serve radically different aesthetics (1530 pirate era vs 100 million BC prehistoric) without any changes. The mechanics stay constant; only the visual theme changes. This validates Giants Drink's world-theming approach.

The time travel premise creates natural player expectations: each new stage will feel different visually while playing identically. This is explicit in the design — the variety is a promise to the player that each stage will have new visual stimulus even if the mechanics are familiar.
